title: Supplier attestation currency — stale SOC 2 / ISO 27001 reports for critical vendors
exclusions: Does NOT evaluate findings in the supplier's report; focuses on currency only.
evidence: Daily 'supplier_attestation_currency' writes expiring/expired attestations to compliance_summary.

compliance.assurance_rationale: DORA Art.28 ICT third-party risk — requires currency of supplier risk information.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that DORA Art.28 (ICT third-party risk) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.44.1: Supplier attestation currency — stale SOC 2 / ISO 27001 reports for critical vendors.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.44.1' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.21(2)(d) supply-chain security — continuous tracking of attestation currency.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(d) (Supply-chain security) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.44.1: Supplier attestation currency — stale SOC 2 / ISO 27001 reports for critical vendors.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.44.1' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: SR-3 supply chain controls — attestations are one of the control artefacts.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIST 800-53 SR-3 (Supply chain controls and processes) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.44.1: Supplier attestation currency — stale SOC 2 / ISO 27001 reports for critical vendors.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.44.1' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: When a critical supplier's SOC 2 or ISO 27001 attestation is within 30 days of expiry or already expired, the UC fires.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Suppliers with a current attestation ≥30 days from expiry do NOT fire.

dataSources: See subcategory preamble. — supplemented by authentication logs, system audit trails, and configuration management database (CMDB) asset inventory exports
spl:
| inputlookup vrm_attestations.csv
| where criticality IN ("critical","high")
| eval days_to_expiry=round((strptime(expires,"%Y-%m-%d")-now())/86400,0)
| where days_to_expiry<=30
| table vendor attestation_type expires days_to_expiry
description: Tracks supplier attestation lifecycles against the TPRM policy and surfaces expiring evidence before the risk committee sees it as an overdue item.
value: Makes vendor-risk currency a live signal rather than an annual gap report.
implementation: (1) Push attestation metadata from OneTrust/ServiceNow VRM into vrm_attestations.csv nightly; (2) run daily; (3) on finding, email supplier contact and assigned procurement lead.
visualization: Table of expiring attestations, Sankey of attestation types per vendor, single-value 'critical vendors with current attestation'.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised compliance operations** — The compliance and governance team performs routine control testing, evidence gathering, and policy reviews that generate legitimate activity matching this use case's detection patterns. These operations are documented in the compliance programme calendar and should be excluded by the compliance-ops service account tag.

2. **Scheduled system maintenance windows** — Approved maintenance activities including patching, upgrades, and configuration changes produce expected control deviations during documented change windows that are pre-approved through the change advisory board (CAB).

3. **External audit and assessment activities** — During periodic audits (internal or external), assessors perform sampling, testing, and evidence collection activities that generate unusual access patterns covered by the engagement letter and audit notification.

4. **Third-party service provider operations** — Contracted service providers performing legitimate operations under signed agreements generate expected activity that may trigger monitoring rules during normal service delivery windows.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `compliance_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved maintenance windows, audit engagement periods, service provider identifiers, and compliance team service accounts. The correlation search joins this lookup and suppresses matches where `exception_status=approved` and the exception remains within its validity period.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Lookups**: `vrm_attestations.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('vrm_attestations.csv')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.28`. The control objective is: "Evidence that DORA Art.28 (ICT third-party risk) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.44.1: Supplier attestation currency — stale SOC 2 / ISO 27001 reports for critical vendors." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into the relevant indexes under the relevant sourcetypes, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


Three operational considerations, authored alongside this UC, apply:


• **Hint 1:** Push attestation metadata from OneTrust/ServiceNow VRM into vrm_attestations.csv nightly. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** run daily. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** on finding, email supplier contact and assigned procurement lead. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "Supplier attestation currency — stale SOC 2 / ISO 27001 reports for critical vendors," the aggregation is the evidence artifact itself — auditors will read these rows as the official record of DORA control activity.

This search begins with `| inputlookup vrm_attestations.csv` — the base dataset is a CSV/KV Store lookup, not live event data. This inverts the usual Splunk assumption: the detection does not stream from an index, it iterates a register. The register itself is the authoritative source of truth (contracts, policies, sign-offs, attestations) and the search joins it against indexed events to find gaps.

Lookup authority: treat `vrm_attestations.csv` as a primary compliance artifact. Its contents ARE the answer the regulator will ask for ('show me your vendor list,' 'show me your CDE inventory,' 'show me your policy exceptions'). Implement the following controls on the lookup itself, not just the search:

• Version the CSV in a git repository with a mandatory pull-request review for every change.
• Maintain a signed-record trail: who changed what, when, and with what approval. A lookup with a silent edit history is an audit liability.
• Automate a nightly diff: `| inputlookup vrm_attestations.csv | eval hash=md5(...) | outputlookup append=true vrm_attestations_audit.csv` — this produces an immutable record of the register's state over time.
• Lock the lookup with role-based access: only the content owner has `list_inputs` + `write_file` on the path; everyone else is read-only.

The live-data side of the join (across the full search range) is the detection trigger — when the register and the telemetry disagree, that's a finding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.44.1: Supplier attestation currency — stale SOC 2 / ISO 27001 repo]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h@h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.44.1,reg=DORA
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `attestation_type`, `days_to_expiry`, `expires`, `vendor`. Run:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table attestation_type, days_to_expiry, expires, vendor
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `days_to_expiry`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(days_to_expiry) as p50 perc90(days_to_expiry) as p90 perc99(days_to_expiry) as p99 max(days_to_expiry) as hi
```

The current threshold `30` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.44.1: DORA evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Table Of Expiring Attestations (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Sankey Of Attestation Types Per Vendor (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Single-Value 'Critical Vendors With Current Attestation' (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**DORA-specific failure modes:**

• Third-party ICT risk register (Art. 28): DORA requires a Register of Information listing every critical ICT third-party service provider. Detections touching supplier risk must reference this register; if a supplier appears in telemetry but not in the Register, that is a Register-completeness finding, not just a technical anomaly.

• Testing cadence (Art. 24-27): DORA's TLPT (Threat-Led Penetration Testing) is required every 3 years for significant institutions. Detections measuring resilience testing must differentiate TLPT (supervisor-led) from self-assessment — mixing them dilutes the evidence.

• Incident classification thresholds (Art. 18): DORA uses specific criteria for 'major' vs 'significant' incidents — client impact, duration, data volume. Detections must classify against these specific thresholds, which differ from GDPR breach criteria; a single event can be major-DORA but not a personal-data breach.

• Resilience testing record retention (Art. 27): results of digital operational resilience tests must be retained. Align log retention to match — typically 5+ years. Verify `frozenTimePeriodInSecs >= 157680000` on any index named by a DORA resilience-testing UC.
grandmaExplanation: We help you see when third-party risk scores move so you can act before a weak vendor becomes a real problem.
